reason: Agarbatti ash is organic and biodegradable, making it suitable for composting.
preparation_steps: Let ash cool completely
Collect in a small container
Mix into compost or garden soil
safety_notes: Ensure ash is fully extinguished before disposal.

reason: Unfired or low-fired clay diyas are made of natural earth and are biodegradable.
preparation_steps: Allow to cool completely
Remove any wax or oil residue
Break into smaller pieces to accelerate decomposition

reason: Stainless steel is highly recyclable and has significant scrap value in India.
preparation_steps: Rinse thoroughly to remove food residue
Dry before disposal
Separate rubber gaskets if present

reason: Coconut shells and husks are organic and compostable, often used in coir production.
preparation_steps: Break into smaller pieces if possible
Can be added directly to a compost bin

reason: Betel leaf and organic paan components are biodegradable organic waste.
preparation_steps: Avoid mixing with non-organic materials
Dispose in green bin or compost
safety_notes: Tobacco-containing paan should be wrapped before disposal to prevent contamination.

reason: Cardboard or paper packaging used for prasad is recyclable if clean and dry.
preparation_steps: Separate food remnants into wet waste
Flatten cardboard
Dispose in blue bin

reason: Jute is a natural biodegradable fibre; old jute bags can be composted or recycled.
preparation_steps: If reusable, donate or repurpose first
Cut into strips to speed up composting
Remove any synthetic liners before composting

reason: Brass is a valuable metal alloy and can be sold to scrap dealers or recycled.
preparation_steps: Clean the item
Take to a local kabadiwala or scrap dealer
Do not discard in regular waste — high scrap value

reason: Thermocol (EPS) is not accepted by most municipal recycling programs in India due to low density and contamination.
preparation_steps: Break into smaller pieces to reduce volume
Dispose in grey/black bin
safety_notes: Do not burn thermocol — releases toxic styrene gases.

reason: Textile waste is recyclable; old clothes can be donated, repurposed, or given to rag pickers.
preparation_steps: Donate to NGOs or rag pickers if usable
If too worn, hand to textile recyclers
Do not mix with wet waste

reason: Newspaper is among the highest-value recyclable paper; widely accepted by kabadiwalas.
preparation_steps: Keep dry
Tie in a bundle
Sell to kabadiwala or place in blue bin

reason: LED and CFL bulbs contain electronic components and trace amounts of mercury; classified as e-waste.
preparation_steps: Handle with care to avoid breakage
Take to authorised e-waste collection centre
Do not mix with regular trash
safety_notes: CFL bulbs contain mercury — if broken, ventilate the room and avoid direct contact with debris.

reason: Expired medicines contain active pharmaceutical ingredients that can contaminate soil and water.
preparation_steps: Do not flush medicines down the toilet
Return to pharmacy take-back programmes if available
Otherwise seal in original packaging and dispose in red bin
safety_notes: Never crush tablets before disposal — this accelerates chemical leaching.

reason: Unglazed terracotta and earthen pottery are natural clay products that safely biodegrade.
preparation_steps: Break into smaller pieces
Can be added to garden soil as drainage material
Or place in green bin
safety_notes: Glazed ceramic pots are non-recyclable — dispose in grey bin instead.

reason: Lead-acid batteries contain sulfuric acid and lead — both hazardous and valuable for recycling.
preparation_steps: Return to authorised battery retailer (exchange scheme available in India)
Never discard in regular waste
Take to e-waste or hazardous waste collection centre
safety_notes: Avoid tilting or cracking the battery casing — acid can cause severe burns.

reason: Multi-layer plastic sachets with oil residue are not recyclable through standard streams.
preparation_steps: Empty remaining oil
Rinse if possible
Dispose in grey/black bin

reason: Used sanitary napkins are biomedical waste and must be segregated to prevent disease transmission.
preparation_steps: Wrap tightly in newspaper or provided pouches
Seal before placing in bin
Dispose in designated black/sanitary bin
safety_notes: Never flush — causes severe drain blockages.

reason: Construction and demolition debris must be segregated and collected separately by municipal C&D waste contractors.
preparation_steps: Segregate from household waste
Contact local municipality for C&D waste pickup
Do not mix with wet or dry household bins
